html:
Beijer Ref is not the tiny post-spin stub described in the prior draft. It is a SEK 63bn refrigeration and HVAC distributor trading around SEK 125 with roughly 507m shares, SEK 10.5bn net debt, and a real enterprise value around SEK 73bn. The opportunity is different: a high-quality compounder has been marked down because reported growth looks tired, FX is masking underlying progress, EQT created an overhang, and short interest is visible at about 3.3% of share capital. That is not deep value, but it is a reasonable setup for a forward earnings reset.

The market is labeling Beijer Ref as a fully valued European HVAC distributor tied to weak heat-pump demand. The better label is a global refrigeration consolidator with a regulatory forced-upgrade cycle, a growing North American platform, and an OEM green-products business beginning to touch data centers, district heating, and industrial energy-efficiency projects.

Q1 2026 was better than the headline. Reported sales fell 4% to SEK 8.55bn, but excluding currency sales rose 3%; EBITA fell 3%, but excluding currency EBITA rose 4%; margin held at 9.4%; EPS was flat at SEK 0.94. Management also said North America would have had positive organic growth excluding weather closures, while OEM grew 5% organically and Fenagy/SCM Frigo entered Q2 with record order books, including roughly SEK 300m of projects tied partly to a data-center project and district heating. That matters because it moves Beijer Ref away from being viewed only as a wholesaler of boxes and refrigerants.

The regulatory tailwind is real. The EU’s revised F-gas regulation applies from 2024 and tightens the HFC quota system, while Beijer Ref says Europe remains behind in the transition to natural refrigerants. The U.S. AIM Act similarly phases down HFC production and consumption by 85% by 2036. This does not create a one-year demand spike; it creates a long replacement, training, stocking, and system-conversion cycle. Beijer Ref is advantaged because fragmented contractors need a distributor that can supply old refrigerants, new lower-GWP refrigerants, natural-refrigerant systems, components, technical training, and inventory availability during the transition.

At SEK 125, consensus has Beijer Ref around 23.5x 2026 earnings and 21x 2027 earnings, with 2026 net income near SEK 2.7bn and 2027 near SEK 3.0bn. That is not optically cheap. The cheapness is in the path if earnings power moves from SEK 5.2 per share in 2026 to SEK 7-8 over the next few years through modest organic growth, margin normalization toward the company’s 10-12% EBITA target, bolt-on M&A, lower interest cost, and private-label/OEM mix. On that view, today’s price is closer to 16-18x medium-term earnings for a business that can compound revenue high single digits to low teens.

The overhangs are visible rather than hidden. EQT sold 20m shares at SEK 157 in August 2025 and still owned about 8.2% of capital afterward, which has likely weighed on the multiple. Short sellers are leaning into the view that this remains an expensive roll-up with weak organic growth. Retail and narrative-driven investors also see the same risk: Beijer Ref is “quality at a price,” and North American margin execution is the swing factor. That is exactly why the next few quarters matter. If organic growth turns positive and North America keeps improving margin mix, the bear case loses its cleanest argument.

Balance sheet risk looks manageable. Net debt to EBITDA excluding IFRS 16 and pensions was 1.93x at Q1, below the company’s 3x ceiling, and Beijer Ref issued SEK 2.5bn of bonds in March 2026 to extend funding flexibility. The dividend is modest at SEK 1.50, so the story is reinvestment and acquisitions, not yield. The risk is not financial distress; the risk is paying too high a multiple for a roll-up whose organic growth stalls.

The base case is that Beijer Ref grows sales from about SEK 37bn in 2025 to SEK 43-45bn by 2028, holds EBITA around 10.8-11.2%, and earns SEK 6.5-7.0 per share. A 25x multiple on SEK 6.8 gives a SEK 170 stock, about 36% upside from SEK 125.

The bull case is that F-gas scarcity, natural refrigerants, OEM projects, and North American private-label mix push EBITA margin above 11.5%, while M&A restarts at a healthier pace. SEK 7.8-8.2 EPS by 2028 at 28-30x gives SEK 225-245, or roughly 80-96% upside.

The bear case is that HVAC weakness persists, North America never earns the hoped-for margin, EQT keeps selling, and the stock de-rates to a normal distributor multiple. SEK 5.0 EPS at 19x gives SEK 95, about 24% downside.

Base target: SEK 170. Bull target: SEK 235. Bear target: SEK 95. Current price: SEK 125.
